source: Ash 2026-05-14 — 'check L3/L2 regularly, archive rotten stuff with notification, weekly review summary' + 'bet on Opus, ship Full'
title: Full memory audit system — detection rules, action ladder, weekly digest, CLI, banner, Telegram
component: memory/audit_rules.py + memory/audit.py + scripts/pi_audit.py + agent/session.py + agent/startup_banner.py + tools/tools_memory.py + tools/tools_obsidian.py + testing/test_audit.py
what_failed: L2 and L3 memory had passive cleanup (T-073 prune_l2_stale, T-078 invalid_at) but no proactive review surface. Stale facts accumulated invisibly; the user had no way to skim what was rotten, approve removals, or restore mistakes. After deprecating L4 (T-079), Ash wanted explicit memory hygiene that he could verify weekly.
where_failed: No single audit module existed. prune_l2_stale ran silently at session exit. No detection beyond time-based age. No digest, no CLI, no notification.
why_likely: Each pruning mechanism shipped tactically (T-026, T-073) without a unified hygiene strategy. Ash asked for the strategic version after the L4 audit exposed the gap.
suggested_fix: Build the full design from the deep-research recommendation: detection rules (stale, heuristic-unconfirmed, invalidated-aged, near-dup, importance-eroded), action ladder (flag → archive → 90d hard-delete), weekly digest at vault/notes/memory/audit/YYYY-Www.md, CLI for keep/delete/restore/merge, terminal banner, Telegram weekly push, golden tests, defense-in-depth importance guard, idempotency. Also fix the three audit-pass bugs found during pre-build (flush_logs missing on exit, Ctrl+C did no cleanup, distill source never persisted to L2).
